title: Topic 351: Why Game Pass Changes Buying Behavior: Access vs. Ownership
content:
# Topic 351: Why Game Pass Changes Buying Behavior: Access vs. Ownership

## Abstract

In an era where the digital landscape is increasingly dominated by subscription services, this paper explores the curious case of the modern gamer, a species once defined by the proud hoarding of plastic-wrapped treasures. We delve into the psychological rabbit hole of Xbox Game Pass, a service that has turned the traditional concept of game ownership on its head. Through a rigorous, albeit frequently satirical, analysis of consumer behavior, we examine the seismic shift from a mindset of "mine" to "mine for now." This paper posits that Game Pass, with its all-you-can-eat buffet of digital delights, has not only altered purchasing habits but has also exposed the delightfully irrational quirks of human psychology. We investigate the paradoxical phenomena of players spending more on games they don't own, the paralysis of choice in a world of endless options, and the ever-present fear of missing out (FOMO) that keeps subscribers tethered to the service. Ultimately, we argue that Game Pass is more than a gaming service; it is a grand social experiment, revealing that when it comes to digital consumption, the illusion of infinite choice is a far more potent and profitable-lure than the tangible satisfaction of ownership.

## Introduction

Once upon a time, in a kingdom not so far away, a gamer's worth was measured by the height of their game library. Towering shelves of plastic cases, each a testament to a conquered quest, a vanquished foe, or at the very least, an optimistic purchase from a Steam sale, were the badges of honor. The sacred ritual of un-sheathing a new game from its cellophane womb, the intoxicating scent of a freshly printed manual—these were the sensory hallmarks of a noble hobby. But then, the winds of change began to blow, carrying with them the whispers of a new paradigm, a siren song of convenience and endless choice. This digital zephyr, known as the subscription model, has now grown into a full-blown hurricane, and at its eye is the behemoth known as Xbox Game Pass.

This paper will embark on a perilous journey into the heart of this storm, seeking to understand how a simple subscription service has so profoundly rewired the gamer's brain. We will explore the psychological underpinnings of the access-versus-ownership debate, a conflict that pits the primal satisfaction of possession against the seductive allure of a limitless digital playground. Is the modern gamer, once a proud dragon hoarding its treasure, now content to be a mere visitor in a digital amusement park, flitting from one ride to the next without a care for the long-term commitment of a season pass? Or is there a more complex, and dare we say, more amusing, psychological drama unfolding? We will dissect the curious case of the Game Pass subscriber, a creature who, despite having access to hundreds of games, often finds themselves spending more money on *other* games, a phenomenon that would make any classical economist weep into their supply and demand charts. This paper will argue that Game Pass is not merely a new way to play games; it is a new way to *be* a gamer, a transformation that reveals more about our own wonderfully flawed human nature than it does about the future of interactive entertainment.

## Literature Review

The hallowed halls of academia have, for decades, been preoccupied with the dusty concept of ownership. Scholars have long waxed lyrical about the "endowment effect," a psychological quirk that makes us value something more simply because we own it [1]. It’s the reason that dusty Beanie Baby collection in your attic feels like a goldmine, and why you’d wrestle a stranger for the last donut in the box, even if you were only vaguely hungry. Ownership, they argue, is tied to our very sense of self, a tangible extension of our identity. To own is to be.

But what happens when ownership becomes… optional? The rise of the subscription economy, a phenomenon that has consumed everything from movies (Netflix) to music (Spotify) and now, our beloved video games, has thrown a rather large, subscription-shaped wrench into the works. Researchers have begun to tentatively poke at this new beast, with studies on digital ownership revealing a fascinating schism in the consumer psyche. Zhu and Cho (2021), in their groundbreaking work, found that consumers who prefer the access model are less concerned with traditional ownership rights, such as the ability to resell or even possess the content indefinitely [2]. It seems the modern consumer is increasingly willing to trade the security of ownership for the intoxicating freedom of access.

Of course, this transition is not without its psychological tripwires. The ever-present specter of “Fear of Missing Out,” or FOMO, has been identified as a key driver of subscription service adoption [3]. The constant influx of new content, coupled with the threat of its eventual departure, creates a low-grade anxiety that can only be soothed by the comforting click of the “subscribe” button. This is the digital equivalent of a limited-time offer, a psychological manipulation so effective it should probably be classified as a dark art. Furthermore, the sheer volume of choice offered by services like Game Pass can lead to a state of “analysis paralysis,” where the user is so overwhelmed by the smorgasbord of options that they end up playing nothing at all, or worse, defaulting to the same familiar game they’ve been playing for years [4]. It’s the digital equivalent of staring into a fridge full of food and declaring there’s nothing to eat.

Thus, the existing literature paints a picture of a consumer in transition, caught between the ingrained psychological comfort of ownership and the exhilarating, yet anxiety-inducing, world of access. The gamer, once a proud collector, is now a digital nomad, wandering a vast and ever-changing landscape of interactive entertainment. The question that remains, and the one this paper will gleefully attempt to answer, is whether this new breed of gamer is a liberated explorer or a lost and bewildered tourist.

## Analysis

At the heart of the Game Pass paradox lies a fascinating psychological tug-of-war. On one side, we have the deeply ingrained human desire for ownership, a primal instinct that whispers sweet nothings about security and control. On the other, we have the siren song of infinite choice, a seductive melody that promises a world without commitment or buyer's remorse. Game Pass, in its infinite wisdom, has managed to exploit both of these desires simultaneously, creating a consumer feedback loop that is as brilliant as it is diabolical.

The service masterfully subverts the endowment effect by offering a low-stakes entry point into the world of gaming. For a monthly fee that is less than the cost of a single new release, players are granted access to a vast library of games. This removes the initial friction of a high-cost purchase, making it easier for players to try games they would otherwise ignore. However, once a player invests time and effort into a game, a new form of psychological ownership begins to take root. The hours spent leveling up a character, the emotional investment in a storyline, the muscle memory of a perfectly executed combo – these all contribute to a sense of ownership that transcends the legal definition. And when that game is eventually removed from the service, the player is faced with a choice: let their investment go to waste, or purchase the game outright, often at a discount. It’s a brilliant bit of psychological jujitsu, turning the fear of loss into a powerful sales tool.

Furthermore, Game Pass has weaponized the concept of FOMO to an unprecedented degree. The constant rotation of games creates a sense of urgency, a feeling that one must play everything *now* before it disappears into the digital ether. This is compounded by the social pressure of online communities, where the conversation is constantly shifting to the latest and greatest Game Pass release. To not be playing the hot new game is to be left out of the conversation, a fate worse than death for the modern, socially-connected gamer. This creates a cycle of perpetual engagement, where the player is constantly chasing the next big thing, never truly satisfied, but always subscribed.

But perhaps the most fascinating aspect of the Game Pass phenomenon is its effect on purchasing behavior *outside* of the service. One might assume that a subscription offering hundreds of games would lead to a decrease in individual game purchases. And yet, the opposite appears to be true. By removing the financial risk of trying new genres and developers, Game Pass acts as a discovery engine, exposing players to a wider variety of games than they would ever encounter on their own. A player who discovers a love for indie platformers through a Game Pass title is now more likely to purchase the next indie platformer that catches their eye, even if it’s not on the service. In this sense, Game Pass is not a replacement for game ownership, but a gateway drug, a tantalizing taste of the vast and wonderful world of interactive entertainment that leaves the player wanting more.

## Real-World Examples

Let us consider the cautionary tale of one “Timmy,” a hypothetical gamer who, for the purposes of this paper, represents the everyman of the digital age. Before the advent of Game Pass, Timmy was a discerning consumer. He would spend weeks researching a new game, poring over reviews, and waiting for the inevitable Steam sale before committing his hard-earned cash. His game library was a curated collection, each title a carefully considered investment. Then came Game Pass. Suddenly, Timmy had access to hundreds of games, a veritable digital candy store. He downloaded *Halo Infinite*, a game he had always been curious about but never enough to purchase. He sank 50 hours into its multiplayer, customizing his Spartan until it was a neon-pink beacon of digital fabulousness. And then, a notification: *Halo Infinite* would be leaving Game Pass in two weeks. The panic set in. All that time, all that effort, all that *pink*. Timmy, a man who once scoffed at paying full price for a game, found himself reaching for his wallet, a victim of the very psychological ownership he had once been immune to.

Or consider the case of “Sarah,” a gamer who had always stuck to the familiar comforts of big-budget, AAA titles. Her gaming diet consisted of a steady stream of *Call of Duty* and *Assassin’s Creed*. Then, on a whim, she downloaded a quirky little indie game called *Vampire Survivors* from Game Pass. It was simple, addictive, and utterly brilliant. Sarah was hooked. She had discovered a whole new genre of gaming she never knew she loved. And when the next highly-anticipated indie roguelike was released, a game *not* on Game Pass, Sarah didn’t hesitate to buy it. Game Pass had not replaced her purchasing habits; it had expanded them, turning her from a picky eater into a gaming gourmand.

And what of the dreaded analysis paralysis? We need only look to the countless Reddit threads and Twitter posts with the title, “I have Game Pass and nothing to play.” These are the digital graveyards of good intentions, filled with gamers who have spent more time scrolling through the Game Pass library than actually playing a game. The sheer volume of choice, once a selling point, has become a burden, a testament to the fact that sometimes, what we want is not more options, but simply a good one.

## Discussion

The curious case of the Game Pass gamer offers a fascinating glimpse into the evolving landscape of consumer psychology. What we are witnessing is not merely a shift in how we purchase video games, but a fundamental re-evaluation of our relationship with digital goods. The transition from a culture of ownership to one of access has profound implications, not just for the gaming industry, but for our broader understanding of human behavior in the digital age. The Game Pass model, with its delicate dance of seduction and subtle coercion, suggests that the desire for status and security, once fulfilled by the accumulation of physical goods, can be effectively replaced by the promise of endless novelty and the fear of being left behind. We are, it seems, becoming a society of digital renters, perpetually chasing the next dopamine hit of a new experience, with little regard for the long-term value of possession.

This raises a number of tantalizing, and slightly unsettling, questions. If we no longer own our games, do we truly own our digital identities? Is the modern gamer a liberated explorer, free to roam the vast plains of interactive entertainment, or a digital serf, tethered to the whims of a corporate landlord? The answer, as with most things in life, is likely a messy and amusing combination of both. The Game Pass phenomenon demonstrates that while we may be shedding the physical trappings of ownership, the psychological need for it remains. We simply find new ways to express it, whether through the time we invest, the communities we join, or the digital trinkets we collect. The game may have changed, but the players, it seems, are as delightfully predictable as ever.

## Conclusion

In the grand, ever-expanding universe of video games, Xbox Game Pass has emerged as a celestial body of immense gravitational pull, warping the very fabric of consumer behavior. It has taken the age-old concept of ownership, with its comforting weight and tangible satisfaction, and replaced it with the ethereal, intoxicating allure of access. The modern gamer, once a proud collector of physical artifacts, has been transformed into a digital nomad, a perpetual tourist in a world of endless, and often overwhelming, choice. This paper has argued that this transformation is not a simple matter of economics, but a complex and frequently hilarious psychological drama. Game Pass, with its masterful manipulation of the endowment effect, its weaponization of FOMO, and its uncanny ability to turn subscribers into paying customers, has revealed a fundamental truth about human nature: we are suckers for a good deal, even if that deal is a carefully constructed illusion. The future of gaming, it seems, is not about owning the games we play, but about being owned by the services that provide them. And as we stand on the precipice of this brave new world, one can only marvel at the beautiful, chaotic, and endlessly entertaining absurdity of it all.
references:
[1] Kahneman, D., Knetsch, J. L., & Thaler, R. H. (1991). Anomalies: The Endowment Effect, Loss Aversion, and Status Quo Bias. *Journal of Economic Perspectives*, 5(1), 193–206.

[2] Zhu, X. A., & Cho, M. (2021). Ownership vs access: consumers’ digital ownership perceptions and preferences. *Aslib Journal of Information Management*, 73(5), 797-813.

[3] Gezgin, D. M. (2023). Deep and surface learning approaches are related to fear of missing out (FoMO) through social media engagement and problematic social media use. *Computers in Human Behavior*, 143, 107703.

[4] Iyengar, S. S., & Lepper, M. R. (2000). When choice is demotivating: Can one desire too much of a good thing?. *Journal of personality and social psychology*, 79(6), 995.
